lessons.title: The audit reframe & the safe first trial
lessons.body:
**Goal:** by the end you can explain *why* the headline use of AI-on-home is the wrong target, name where the real value is, and describe the safe first thing to actually do.

Picture the demo everyone shows: *"Hey Claude, turn on the lounge lights."* It's a magic trick — and it's the **least** useful thing an LLM does for your home, for two reasons. First, you've already got that: a $0 local voice intent engine flips lights instantly without any LLM. Second, the moment you ask the LLM to *write* config — *"build me an automation that…"* — it produces YAML and Jinja templates that are **subtly wrong** often enough that you can't trust them unchecked. The flashy uses are the weakest.

So where's the gold? In **reading and reasoning over a sprawling configuration you can't hold in your head** — i.e. **audit and debug**. *"Why does this automation misfire?"* (an LLM that can read execution **traces** spots the failing condition in seconds). *"What in my config is deprecated?"* *"Consolidate these five near-identical scripts."* *"Is my nightly backup actually running, or silently failing?"* That last one is the quiet killer of self-hosted setups — a job that errors silently, a sensor that went `unavailable` weeks ago — and an LLM with **read access** surfaces it proactively. Even "build me an automation" is really an audit task wearing a hat: your job is to **review the draft**, not type the YAML.

Which tells you exactly how to start — and to start *safe*. The on-ramp is a **read-only config audit**: run the community `ha-mcp` server in **Read-Only Mode**, point it at your config, traces, history and logs, and ask *"what's broken, deprecated, or silently failing?"* It's the lowest-risk, highest-value, fully-reversible first move. You give the LLM **nothing writable** and it still earns its keep on day one. (Setup detail comes in L2–L5; the principle is: read before you let it write.)
lessons.check: *Your friend says "AI on Home Assistant is pointless — I can already turn on lights with my voice." What's the better use you'd point them to, and what's the safe first thing to do?*

lessons.title: The two connection paths
lessons.body: Native MCP Server (Assist API, control-only) vs community `ha-mcp` (REST+WS, audit/build, 84 tools, traces); the context-cost knob.

lessons.title: Local vs cloud & the privacy fork
lessons.body: Local MCP proxy vs public HA; connection-location vs model-location; default-to-local.

lessons.title: Claude vs a local LLM
lessons.body: Ollama + tool-capable Qwen3; local voice (Wyoming/Whisper/Piper); the trade-off matrix; best-per-job.

lessons.title: Safe operating patterns
lessons.body: The five guardrails; what NEVER to expose; earning write access one reviewed task at a time.

lessons.title: Risks & staying current
lessons.body: Prompt injection via untrusted data; MCP/STDIO disclosures; LLM config errors; why this set refreshes monthly.

quiz.q: Where is the real value of AI on a smart home — and what's the trap?
quiz.a: Value = **audit/debug** (why an automation misfires, deprecated config, silent failures, refactors). Trap = treating "turn on a light" / "build me an automation" as the prize — least valuable, most error-prone. [wiki/01 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: Name the two connection paths from an LLM to Home Assistant and the key difference.
quiz.a: (a) HA's **native MCP Server** (via the **Assist API** — control/query *exposed* entities only); (b) community **`ha-mcp`** (via **REST + WebSocket** — adds config, automations, traces, dashboards: build/audit). [wiki/02 · v7.3.0]

quiz.q: Which path lets an LLM read automation traces and audit config, and why does that matter?
quiz.a: **`ha-mcp`** (deep config search + traces + logs). It matters because the audit reframe (Q1) needs *reading* the config, which the native control-only path can't do. [wiki/02 · v7.3.0]

quiz.q: The privacy fork: when does pointing Claude at HA require HA to be public, and how do you avoid it?
quiz.a: A **remote custom connector** is brokered through Anthropic's cloud → HA must be **public**. Avoid it with a **local MCP proxy** (`mcp-proxy` + a long-lived token) → HA stays on-LAN. [wiki/03 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: A local MCP proxy keeps the *connection* on-LAN. Does it keep your home *data* off the cloud?
quiz.a: **No** — if the model is **Claude**, state/prompts still go to Anthropic's API to be reasoned over. The proxy fixes topology, not model location. For data-on-network too you need a **local LLM**. [wiki/03, wiki/04 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: Claude vs a local LLM for home control — the recommended split?
quiz.a: **Best-per-job:** local model (Ollama + tool-capable **Qwen3**) for the always-on control/voice layer (privacy + offline); **Claude** for deep audit/refactor over a local connection. Not one-tool-for-all. [wiki/04 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: Which models can control Home Assistant via Ollama?
quiz.a: **Only models that support Tools.** Control is an experimental feature scoped by exposed entities. [wiki/04 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: Name the fully-local voice stack.
quiz.a: **Wyoming** protocol gluing **Whisper/faster-whisper** or **Speech-to-Phrase** (STT) + **Piper** (TTS) + optional small local LLM via Assist. No audio leaves the house. [wiki/04 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: The five guardrails for letting an LLM near a real home?
quiz.a: **Read-only by default · draft-not-deploy · least-privilege exposure · local-first topology · treat all device/state text as untrusted data (not instructions).** [wiki/05 · v7.3.0]

quiz.q: What's the safe first trial, and what should you NEVER expose?
quiz.a: Safe first trial = a **read-only config audit** (`ha-mcp` Read-Only Mode). Never expose: live credentials, **locks/alarms/garage/gates**, structural/destructive tools (recorder, OS/Supervisor, writable backups) to an autonomous loop. [wiki/05 · v7.3.0]

quiz.q: Why is "treat device data as untrusted" a security rule, not a slogan?
quiz.a: Device names, entity states, calendar/camera/notification text are attacker-influenceable; instructions hidden there are **indirect prompt injection**. OWASP: tool returns are *data, not instructions*. [wiki/06 · 2026]

quiz.q: What changed in `ha-mcp` v7.3.0 that proves why this set refreshes monthly?
quiz.a: `ha_config_set_yaml` was **moved to beta** (now needs a feature flag + the custom component) — a real breaking change in a fast-moving dependency. [wiki/02, wiki/06 · v7.3.0]

quiz.q: Why does an *open-book* quiz capture little of the testing effect?
quiz.a: Because the benefit comes from **effortful retrieval from memory** — if the answer is in front of you, you don't retrieve, so you get comprehension but not the retention gain. [wiki/02 · high]

quiz.q: State Cepeda's rule of thumb for the optimal spacing gap.
quiz.a: The optimal inter-study gap is roughly **10–20% of the target retention interval** (falling toward ~5% for a one-year horizon) — so longer retention needs longer gaps. [wiki/02 · high · meta-analysis 2006]

quiz.q: Name Miller's four tiers and what each assessment format reaches.
quiz.a: **Knows** (facts/MCQ) → **Knows how** (vignette MCQ, reasoning) → **Shows how** (OSCE/sim) → **Does** (workplace audit). [wiki/03 · established-theory]

quiz.q: Moore's seven levels — where does attendance CPD sit, and where does a spaced closed-book retrieval reach?
quiz.a: Attendance = levels **1–2** (participation/satisfaction); spaced closed-book retrieval = level **3 retention**, edging to **4 (competence)**. CTC + audit reaches **5–6**. [wiki/03 · established-theory]

quiz.q: What did Davis et al. (2006, JAMA) establish, and what's the design implication?
quiz.a: Physicians have **limited ability to self-assess** vs external measures — so don't rely on self-selected need/self-certified competence; supply external signal and build calibration. [wiki/03 · high · systematic-review]

quiz.q: Per Cervero & Gaines, what five design features make CME most effective?
quiz.a: **Interactive · multiple methods · multiple exposures · longer · focused on outcomes that matter.** [wiki/04 · moderate · systematic-review 2015]

quiz.q: Forsetlund (Cochrane 2021): didactic vs interactive meetings?
quiz.a: **Interactive workshops** can produce moderately large practice change; **didactic sessions alone are unlikely** to change practice. [wiki/04 · moderate · systematic-review]

quiz.q: What is a commitment-to-change prompt and why is it high-value?
quiz.a: Asking "what will you do differently?" with follow-up — stated commitments **predict practice change**; cheap way to climb from Moore 3 to 5, and it makes an auditable artefact. [wiki/04 · moderate · primary-study]

quiz.q: Why is certainty-based marking a good fit for *expert* clinicians specifically?
quiz.a: Experts' risk is **confident, out-of-date practice**, not ignorance; CBM surfaces the "confidently wrong" zone and improves calibration. [wiki/03 · moderate · primary-study]

quiz.q: What is mastery learning, and how should it gate full CPD credit?
quiz.a: A **fixed high standard with variable attempts** — keep going (with feedback and parallel items) until criterion. Gate full credit on a **mastery threshold with spaced re-attempts on fresh items**, not a one-shot low pass. [wiki/03 · moderate · systematic-review]

quiz.q: What's the difference between blocking and interleaving, and when does interleaving help?
quiz.a: Blocking = one topic at a time; interleaving = mixing related topics — it improves **discrimination/transfer** (most when items are confusable), at a cost in immediate fluency. [wiki/02 · moderate]

quiz.q: Across AU/US/India/China, what stays the same and what changes?
quiz.a: The **learning science is universal**; only the **credit shell** (accounting unit + outcome orientation + accrediting body) changes — build it as a per-market config. [wiki/06 · regulatory-current]

lessons.title: Lesson 1
lessons.body: AMC = non-progressive congenital contractures, ≥2 joints in ≥2 regions; a final common path, not one disease. Split that matters surgically: **amyoplasia** (sporadic, muscle replaced, symmetric) vs **distal arthrogryposis** (genetic/AD, hands+feet, muscle better preserved). Muscle presence is the hinge of every later decision. → KB 01.
lessons.check: Why does it matter at the operating table whether a child has amyoplasia vs DA?

lessons.title: Lesson 2
lessons.body: Useful mobility, not maximum mobility. UL goal priorities: hand-to-mouth, grasp/release, perineal care, bimanual. Bilateral rule: one elbow flexes, one extends. Assess passive vs active ROM and, above all, **muscle presence**. Counsel on function — PROMs stay low, QoL is normal. → KB 02.
lessons.check: A bilateral patient — what's the trap in making both elbows flex?

lessons.title: Lesson 3
lessons.body: Stretching, **serial casting** (best gains after first cast; DA responds, classic AML ~75% recurrence), splints/orthoses, elbow flexion-assist orthosis; botulinum toxin = low-evidence adjunct. Therapy repositions/maintains but can't make muscle. → KB 03.
lessons.check: When does recurrent wrist deformity after casting change your plan?

lessons.title: Lesson 4
lessons.body: Step 1 posterior release ± triceps lengthening for **passive** flexion — **earlier is better (<2 y, ~88° arc)**; release alone beats simultaneous osteotomy for arc. Step 2 active flexion (triceps-to-biceps / bipolar LD ~97.7°, 92% satisfied / Steindler / pec major) **only with passive arc + donor**. Step 3 FFMT (gracilis) if no donor. → KB 04.
lessons.check: Name the prerequisites before you promise a child active elbow flexion.

lessons.title: Lesson 5
lessons.body: Shoulder: external-rotation humeral osteotomy reorients the hand. Wrist: casting → **dorsal/intercarpal carpal wedge osteotomy ± FCU/ECU→ECRB transfer** (± volar release; PRC if severe) for rigid flexion >~40°/ulnar deviation — and know **whom not to operate**. → KB 04–05.
lessons.check: Why combine a bony osteotomy with a tendon transfer rather than either alone?

lessons.title: Lesson 6
lessons.body: Thumb-in-palm = first-web contracture + MCPJ instability + weak extensors + tight FPL. À la carte: first-web release + **skin flap (Abdel-Ghani)** + **MCPJ chondrodesis** (static stabilisation when muscles can't be transferred) ± FPL lengthening. Camptodactyly: therapy first, modest surgical gains. → KB 06.
lessons.check: Why chondrodesis rather than a tendon transfer for the amyoplasia thumb MCPJ?

lessons.title: Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Whole field is Therapeutic IV–V; 2026 systematic review = the anchor. Walk the algorithm: therapy → shoulder → elbow → wrist → thumb → fingers, repositioning into usable arcs and powering only where muscle exists. → KB 07.
**Capstone:** Build a staged UL plan for a child with bilateral amyoplasia (extended elbows, flexed wrists, thumbs-in-palm) and justify each step + its evidence level.

lessons.title: Lesson 1
lessons.body:
**Goal:** understand what the box does, then stand up the HVAC layer of your HA hub — locally, reversibly, with no credential exposure.

### 1.1 The mental model (2 minutes)
Your CoolMaster is a **universal HVAC gateway**. VRF/VRV systems (Daikin, Mitsubishi, Fujitsu, etc.) each run a *proprietary* communication loop between the outdoor and indoor units. Historically you needed a different mediation box per brand. CoolMaster speaks all of them and presents every indoor unit through **one interface**. [wiki/01]

Two facts you'll use constantly:
- **Lines.** Your unit has **8 HVAC line connectors, L1–L8.** Each line is a bus that can carry one brand and its indoor units. One box, mixed brands across lines. [wiki/01]
- **Unit IDs.** Every indoor unit is named **`Ln.XYY`** — line, dot, indoor number. Example: `L1.001`. This same ID shows up in HA, BACnet, KNX, and the ASCII protocol. [wiki/02]

### 1.2 The one diagnostic that tells you everything: `ls` (5 minutes)
Before touching Home Assistant, prove the gateway is talking to your HVAC. The gateway runs a plain-text (ASCII) command server over TCP. Connect to it and run **`ls`**.

- Find the gateway's **IP** (its display shows it; or check UniFi's client list). 
- From a computer on the same LAN, open a raw TCP/telnet-style socket to the gateway's ASCII port (the HA path uses **port 10102** — see the note below) and type:
  ```
  ls
  ```
- You should get one line per indoor unit, e.g.:
  ```
  L1.100 ON 19C 30C High Fan OK # 0
  L1.101 OFF 28C 23C High Cool OK - 0
  ```
  That's UID · on/off · room temp · setpoint · fan · mode · status. [wiki/02]

**Read the result:**
- **Units appear** → the gateway↔HVAC link is healthy and you know exactly which brands/units are on which lines. Integration will be a formality. ✅
- **Nothing appears** → the problem is the HVAC line config/wiring or the gateway's brand driver — *not* Home Assistant. Fix that first (device display / ToolBox), then continue. [wiki/06]

> _Port note:_ **10102** is the verified port the Home Assistant library uses. If a raw socket on 10102 doesn't answer, check the device's ASCII-server port in its display/ToolBox config (this is an open verification item — see [[discovery-log]] Q4). [wiki/02]

### 1.3 Reserve the IP (1 minute)
In UniFi, give the CoolMaster a **DHCP reservation** so its IP never changes. A changed lease is the #1 cause of "HA lost my HVAC." [wiki/03]

### 1.4 Add the Home Assistant integration (3 minutes)
Now the easy part — it's purely local, no cloud, no PIN.
1. HA → **Settings → Devices & Services → Add Integration**.
2. Search **"CoolMasterNet"** and select it. (Yes — "Net", even though your unit is the newer family; that's just the integration's name.) [wiki/03]
3. Enter the gateway's **IP** (and **port 10102** if asked). [wiki/03]
4. HA creates a **climate entity per indoor unit** (`Ln.XYY`). You now have per-room temp, setpoint, on/off, fan (low/med/high/auto), and mode (auto/cool/dry/fan/heat) in HA. [wiki/03]

### 1.5 The boundaries you just respected (1 minute)
- **No cloud.** The integration is **Local Polling** — it never needed your CoolRemote account. [wiki/05]
- **No PIN.** Your CoolRemote registration PIN is a *credential* for the vendor cloud — Home Assistant doesn't use it, and you never typed it anywhere here. [wiki/05]
- **No firmware update.** v0.9.7A integrates as-is over the stable ASCII protocol. Update later only if you hit a specific need — and back up first. [wiki/06]
- **Reversible.** If anything's off, delete the integration; nothing on the gateway changed. [wiki/03]

### 1.6 Check your understanding
1. What does `Ln.XYY` mean, and what does `ls` show you? 
2. Does the HA integration need the internet or your CoolRemote PIN? 
3. If `ls` returns nothing, is that an HA problem or an HVAC-side problem?

(Answers: wiki/02 · wiki/03+05 · wiki/06.) When you can answer these, you've finished Lesson 1 — your HVAC is in HA, locally. ✅

---

lessons.title: Lesson 2
lessons.body: The ASCII command language: command rules (`<CR><LF>`, case-sensitive), the full `ls`/`ls+`/`props` family, the per-unit setters, RS232 vs the TCP `aserver`, and Virtual Addresses for BACnet/Modbus. Hands-on: drive a unit by hand over the socket, then watch it change in HA. → wiki/02.

lessons.title: Lesson 3
lessons.body: CoolMaster vs CoolMasterPro vs the discontinued CoolMasterNet; EDGE/LT/Home siblings; the 32-unit cap; which brand is on each of your L1–L8 lines (from your `ls` output); brand-adapter gotchas (Fujitsu UTY-VGGX, Hitachi PSC-6RAD). → wiki/01.

lessons.title: Lesson 4
lessons.body: When the native HA integration isn't the answer: sharing units with a BMS (BACnet), a KNX layer, Modbus, and the Savant IP driver. Why these are *reserve* paths for the estate, and the D1/D2 lever — CoolMaster is hub-agnostic, so HVAC isn't locked to Savant. → wiki/04.

lessons.title: Lesson 5
lessons.body: CoolRemote cloud vs local control; the local-first verdict (D5); whether to keep or remove the unit's cloud registration (your call, your PIN); never exposing the gateway to the internet; using HA's own remote path instead of a per-vendor cloud. → wiki/05.

lessons.title: Lesson 6
lessons.body:
The firmware question (v0.9.7A → 1.x: when and how, via ToolBox, with a backup); no-Matter-yet and what that means; known limits (poll-not-push, residential telemetry gap, surface gaps); the troubleshooting first-moves; how the quarterly refresh keeps this set current. → wiki/06.

---

quiz.q: What does a CoolMaster actually do, in one line?
quiz.a: It's a **universal HVAC gateway** — speaks each VRF/VRV brand's proprietary indoor-unit loop natively and presents all units through one unified interface to an automation/BMS system. [wiki/01 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: CoolMaster vs CoolMasterPro vs CoolMasterNet?
quiz.a: **CoolMaster** = residential, up to **32** units. **CoolMasterPro** = commercial, up to **256** units + local service-data access. **CoolMasterNet** = **discontinued** (replaced by the Product Line) but **still firmware-supported** and the **name the HA integration uses**. [wiki/01 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: What's the unit-addressing format, and what does `ls` do?
quiz.a: **`Ln.XYY`** (Line.Indoor), e.g. `L1.001`; `Ln` = HVAC line L1–L8. **`ls`** lists the visible indoor units the gateway has detected (UID, on/off, temps, fan, mode, status). [wiki/02 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Which HA integration, what IoT class, and does it need the cloud?
quiz.a: The native **`coolmaster`** ("CoolMasterNet") integration; **Local Polling**; **no cloud / no CoolRemote account** needed. [wiki/03 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: What TCP port does the HA path use, and what library backs it?
quiz.a: Default **port 10102**, via **`pycoolmasternet-async`**. [wiki/03 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: What does each HA climate entity expose?
quiz.a: Current temp, target temp (setpoint), on/off, **fan** (low/med/high/auto), **mode** (auto/cool/dry/fan/heat). [wiki/03 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Which building/automation protocols does CoolMaster speak?
quiz.a: **Modbus RTU/IP, BACnet IP/MSTP, KNX, ASCII, REST, HDL** — and native drivers for Savant/Control4/Crestron/RTI/Vantage/Dynalite etc. **No Matter.** [wiki/04, wiki/06 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Does CoolMaster support Matter? Architecture consequence?
quiz.a: **No** (as of 2026-06, verified by absence). So HVAC joins HA as its **own native integration**, not via a Matter bridge — independent of the Dynalite-Matter route (D3). [wiki/06 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Why does CoolMaster *strengthen* the HA-as-hub case (D1) and the Savant-retire lean (D2)?
quiz.a: It speaks **both** Savant's IP driver **and** the native HA integration over the LAN — HVAC is **hub-agnostic, not Savant-locked**, so moving the brain to HA strands nothing. [wiki/04 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Scott's unit is fw **v0.9.7A** and cloud-registered. Must he update firmware or give HA his PIN to integrate?
quiz.a: **Neither.** The HA integration uses the stable ASCII protocol — v0.9.7A integrates as-is (update only for a needed fix, with a backup). The **PIN is a CoolRemote credential**, irrelevant to the local integration. [wiki/05, wiki/06 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Fastest safe path to get the unit into HA (the four moves)?
quiz.a: (1) Reserve the device IP on UniFi; (2) socket in, run **`ls`** to confirm units; (3) add the **CoolMasterNet** integration with IP + port 10102; (4) confirm a climate entity per room. Local, reversible, no credential exposure. [wiki/03 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: One honest limitation of the HA integration?
quiz.a: **Local Polling, not push** — wall-controller changes show after the next poll; and some HVAC extras (swing/vane, advanced diagnostics) may not surface. [wiki/03, wiki/06 · 2026-06]

lessons.title: Lesson 1
lessons.body:
**Read:** `kb/01`. **Goal:** triage before you diagnose.
- Minimise distress (it worsens obstruction); focused exam on the carer's lap.
- Toxic vs non-toxic split; the two tells — **cough → croup, drool → epiglottitis**.
- Stridor *loudness* ≠ severity; falling air entry and a tiring child are the danger.
- Investigations: usually none — croup is clinical.
**Self-check:** Q1–Q2 in quiz.

lessons.title: Lesson 2
lessons.body:
**Read:** `kb/02`. **Goal:** own the core.
- Subglottic oedema + cricoid ring = big resistance rise; parainfluenza commonest; night-worse, peaks night 2–3.
- Severity table (mild→life-threatening); Westley score.
- **Steroid for nearly all (dexamethasone default, AU 0.15 mg/kg; 0.6 for severe); adrenaline fast-but-transient, observe 3 h.**
- What has no role: antibiotics, humidified air, cough suppressants, routine heliox.
**Self-check:** Q3–Q6.

lessons.title: Lesson 3
lessons.body:
**Read:** `kb/03`. **Goal:** know when the label changes the plan (mostly it doesn't, acutely).
- Spasmodic = sudden nocturnal, no prodrome; **treat identically** to viral croup.
- Recurrent/atypical (or <6 mo / >6 yr) = referral trigger → exclude subglottic stenosis/haemangioma, reflux.
**Self-check:** Q7.

lessons.title: Lesson 4
lessons.body:
**Read:** `kb/04`, `kb/05`. **Goal:** recognise the toxic airway emergency.
- Epiglottitis: Hib-era rarity (4.9→0.02/100k); toxic, drool, no cough, tripod; **airway team, hands off the throat**.
- Bacterial tracheitis: "croup that won't quit", toxic, **no response to adrenaline**, *S. aureus*, often intubated.
**Self-check:** Q8–Q10.

lessons.title: Lesson 5
lessons.body:
**Read:** `kb/06`, `kb/07`. **Goal:** age-split the abscesses; get the ARF exception.
- RPA young (<6, torticollis) vs PTA adolescent (trismus, uvular deviation); CT decides drainage.
- GAS pharyngitis: score → swab → penicillin; modest benefit — **except the AU ARF exception** in high-risk children.
**Self-check:** Q11–Q13.

lessons.title: Lesson 6
lessons.body:
**Read:** `kb/08`, `kb/09`. **Goal:** the non-infective mimic + the master table.
- FB: <3 yr, abrupt while eating/playing, afebrile, unilateral signs; normal CXR doesn't exclude; rigid bronchoscopy.
- Run the discriminator table cold; recite the two tells + the non-response rule; name the AU divergences.
**Self-check:** Q14–Q16 + the synthesis challenge.

---

**On completion:** offer a **spaced review** — re-test the quiz at day 1, day 3, week 1, month 1 (Leitner), and revisit Lesson 4 (the emergencies) preferentially. Then point back to the expert: *"Ask the croup expert: …"* for applied questions.

lessons.title: What Dynalite is & the suite
lessons.body:
**Goal:** by the end you can say what Dynalite is, name the lifecycle tools, and place System Builder in them.

Dynalite (now **Signify Dynalite**) is a professional lighting-control system: intelligent devices — controllers, sensors, wall/touch interfaces, gateways — talk over the **DyNet** network and are programmed from PC software. The software is a **suite that follows a project's life**: **System Designer** lays it out and produces a Bill of Materials; **System Builder** installs, programs and **commissions** it; **System Manager** (and the Cloud) run and maintain it afterwards. The clever part: a **single job file** flows through all three, so there's one source of truth from design to daily operation. System Builder — the focus of this course — is the **commissioning** tool in the middle.
lessons.check: *Where does System Builder sit in the lifecycle, and what carries through all the tools?*

lessons.title: DyNet & the four concepts
lessons.body: The network; areas → channels → presets → tasks.

lessons.title: The hardware you program
lessons.body: Controllers, sensors, UIs, gateways; DALI relationship.

lessons.title: The System Builder workflow (5.49)
lessons.body: Ribbon UI, wizards, Dynalite Assist; design→configure→program→commission→test.

lessons.title: Integration & management
lessons.body: Dynalite ↔ Home Assistant; BMS/DALI; System Manager & Cloud.

lessons.title: Staying current
lessons.body: The version line; legacy tools to distrust; how this set keeps you current.

quiz.q: Name the Pro Workflow lifecycle tools in order and what System Builder does.
quiz.a: **System Designer** (design + BoM) → **System Builder** (install/**commission/program**) → **System Manager / Multiroom** (manage) — all sharing **one job file**. [wiki/01]

quiz.q: The four core programming concepts in System Builder?
quiz.a: **Areas → Channels → Presets → Tasks** (zone → load output → stored scene → logic/automation). [wiki/02]

quiz.q: What is a *preset*?
quiz.a: A **stored scene** — a set of channel levels recalled together; the primary way lighting states are driven. [wiki/02]

quiz.q: What is DyNet, and what's a *gateway* for?
quiz.a: DyNet = Dynalite's control network/protocol (DyNet 1 serial / DyNet 2 IP-friendly). A **gateway** bridges DyNet to IP/DALI/BMS/third-party (the integration point). [wiki/02, wiki/03]

quiz.q: Current System Builder version and its headline change?
quiz.a: **5.49** — **ribbon-based toolbar** + **Dynalite Assist** (a shift from the pre-5.x toolbox/menu UI). [wiki/04, wiki/06]

quiz.q: A YouTube guide's menus don't match your System Builder. Why?
quiz.a: It's likely **pre-5.x toolbox UI**; the **5.x ribbon** replaced that layout. Concepts hold, navigation doesn't — check docs.dynalite.com 5.49. [wiki/06]

quiz.q: How does Dynalite get into Home Assistant?
quiz.a: Via the HA **Dynalite integration** over **IP**, through a **gateway** exposing DyNet over Ethernet; areas/channels appear as HA light entities. [wiki/05]

quiz.q: The commissioning loop to internalise?
quiz.a: *areas → channels → presets → tasks → bind UIs/sensors → download to devices → test/tune.* [wiki/04]

quiz.q: Why keep the single job file sacred?
quiz.a: It's the **one source of truth** carried Designer→Builder→Manager; re-keying downstream breaks consistency. [wiki/01, wiki/05]

quiz.q: Legacy tools to be wary of in old guides?
quiz.a: **DLight / Picasso / Dynalite Toolbox / EnvisionProject** and the pre-ribbon System Builder UI — `deprecated` for current UI steps (incl. many official videos). [wiki/06]

quiz.q: What do you physically need to connect a (DyNet1) Dynalite system to Home Assistant?
quiz.a: An **RS485-to-IP device** on the bus (almost any works), network on **DyNet1 (not DyNet2)**, and your **area/channel map** (or HA `autodiscover`, turned off afterwards). [wiki/05]

quiz.q: Where does the real Dynalite knowledge live (given thin written docs)?
quiz.a: In the **official training**: System Builder Training Guides, **Installers Training Videos** (@SignifyDynalite / PhilipsDynalite), and **Online Certification** — video/courses are the documentation substitute. [wiki/07]

lessons.title: What HA is & how to run it
lessons.body:
**Goal:** by the end you can say what HA is, pick an install method, and know whether you can run add-ons.

Home Assistant is an open-source, **local-first** home-automation hub: it runs on *your* hardware and connects devices across ecosystems through integrations, so your automations keep working without the cloud. There are four ways to install it — **HAOS** (the all-in-one appliance image, recommended for most), **Supervised** (Core + Supervisor on your own Debian), **Container** (just the Docker image), and **Core** (bare Python). The fork in the road: **only HAOS and Supervised can run add-ons** (the containerised extras like Zigbee2MQTT and the backup system). Container and Core give you the engine but none of that scaffolding.
lessons.check: *You want the easy path with add-ons and built-in backups — which install method, and why?*

lessons.title: Entities, areas, and your first automation
lessons.body: The entity model; trigger/condition/action; any-zone + `for`-duration.

lessons.title: Integrations & interoperability
lessons.body: The integration model; Matter/Thread/Zigbee/Z-Wave/SmartThings as HA integrations; local-vs-cloud.

lessons.title: Dashboards, voice, ESPHome
lessons.body: Sections/cards; Assist (local voice); building your own sensors.

lessons.title: Operating it safely
lessons.body: Backups, the update ritual, remote access, security, your credential boundary.

lessons.title: Staying current
lessons.body: The release train; the 6-month deprecation policy; reading the deprecation register; how this set keeps *you* current.

quiz.q: Name the four HA installation methods, and which two can run add-ons.
quiz.a: HAOS, Supervised, Container, Core — only **HAOS** and **Supervised** run add-ons. [wiki/01 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: What's the release cadence and version scheme?
quiz.a: A major release the **first Wednesday of each month**, versioned `YYYY.MM`, with mid-month patches. [wiki/06 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: How long is HA's deprecation window before removal?
quiz.a: ~**6 months** from `deprecated` to `removed`. [wiki/06 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: A guide's template sensor uses `platform: template` under `sensor:`. What happens on 2026.6 and what's the fix?
quiz.a: **Removed** in 2026.6 (deprecated 2025.12) — breaks. Move to the top-level **`template:`** block. [wiki/02, wiki/06 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: What replaced the deprecated `device_tracker.see` action?
quiz.a: **Template entities** now support the **device_tracker** platform. [wiki/02 · 2026.5]

quiz.q: SmartThings in HA — controller or integration, local or cloud?
quiz.a: A **cloud integration**: HA connects to a SmartThings account to surface its devices — not running SmartThings itself. Prefer local integrations where possible. [wiki/03 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: The one habit before every update?
quiz.a: **Take a backup** (encrypted, 2026.4+) and **read the breaking-changes section** first. [wiki/05, wiki/06 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: Konnected hardware after 2026.6 — what now?
quiz.a: The integration was **removed** (deprecated 2025.10); **flash the hardware to ESPHome**. [wiki/03, wiki/06 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: Safest remote-access options, and what to avoid?
quiz.a: Nabu Casa Cloud / VPN (WireGuard/Tailscale) / reverse proxy + TLS. **Avoid raw port-forwarding.** [wiki/05 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: New in 2026.6 automations (two things)?
quiz.a: Triggers/conditions work with **any defined zone** (not just home); **`for` duration** field on triggers/conditions. [wiki/02 · 2026.6]

lessons.title: What Matter actually is
lessons.body:
**Goal:** by the end you can say what Matter *is* (and isn't), name the data-model layers, and explain why "Matter-compatible" doesn't guarantee a feature works.

Matter is an **application layer** for the smart home — a shared *language* devices speak so a certified gadget works across Apple, Google, Amazon and Samsung. The single most important thing to get straight: **Matter is not a new radio or network.** It runs **over IP**, on the Wi-Fi, Ethernet, or **Thread** you already have. It standardises the *device language* on top, nothing below.

That language is layered. A **node** is an addressable device. Inside it are **endpoints** (functional units — a power strip is one node with many outlet endpoints). Each endpoint has a **device type** (e.g. *On/Off Light*, *Thermostat*) which mandates certain **clusters** — bundles of capability (the *On/Off* cluster, the *Level Control* cluster for dimming, the *Color Control* cluster). Inside clusters live **attributes** (state, like brightness 0–254), **commands** (actions, like Toggle), and **events** (notifications). Finally, a **fabric** is a security domain — the set of devices that trust each other; commissioning a device into an ecosystem adds it to that ecosystem's fabric.

Here's the payoff and the trap in one: **capability lives at the cluster level.** A light advertised as "Matter-compatible" that only implements the On/Off cluster **won't dim over Matter** even though its own app dims it fine. So "Matter-compatible" is never a yes/no — it's "*which clusters?*" This is exactly why Matter's marketing can over-deliver on paper and under-deliver in your hallway, a theme we'll hit hard in L4 and L6.
lessons.check: *Your new "Matter" blind connects to Home Assistant but you can only open/close it fully — no partial positioning. In Matter's data model, what's the likely cause?*

lessons.title: Getting devices on: commissioning & multi-admin
lessons.body: QR/BLE/**NFC (1.6)** flows; the onboarding payload; **multi-admin** vs **Joint Fabric (1.6)**.

lessons.title: How it travels: Thread, Wi-Fi & bridges
lessons.body: The three transports; BLE/NFC = commissioning only; Thread Border Routers; **bridges** (Hue, **Dynalite**).

lessons.title: Who runs it: ecosystems & the reality gap
lessons.body: Apple/Google/Amazon/SmartThings/**HA** as controllers; "Works with Matter" ≠ "all features work"; the version-lag problem.

lessons.title: Certification & the version train
lessons.body: The cert program + Product Security 1.1; what each release (1.0→1.6) added; spec vs SDK vs controller — the three clocks.

lessons.title: Limits & staying current
lessons.body: The honest "Matter still can't…" register; how the monthly refresh tracks the spec/SDK/platform churn and keeps *you* current.

quiz.q: What *layer* is Matter, and what does it run over?
quiz.a: An **application layer over IP** — it standardises the device language; it runs over **Wi-Fi, Ethernet, and Thread**. It doesn't replace your network. [wiki/01 · 1.6]

quiz.q: Put these in order, smallest to largest: cluster, node, attribute, endpoint, device type.
quiz.a: **attribute → cluster → device type → endpoint → node.** Attributes/commands live in clusters; a device type mandates clusters; endpoints group device types; a node is the addressable device. [wiki/01 · 1.6]

quiz.q: Why can a "Matter-compatible" dimmable light fail to dim over Matter?
quiz.a: Capability lives at the **cluster** level — if it only exposes On/Off and not **Level Control**, it won't dim over Matter even though its own app does. [wiki/01, wiki/06 · 1.6]

quiz.q: What are the two headline features of Matter 1.6, and the date/event it launched?
quiz.a: **NFC commissioning** (full, incl. *before the device has power*) and **Joint Fabric** (co-administered single fabric). Released **2026-06-17** at CSA's **"Unify"** conference, Austin. [wiki/02, wiki/05 · 1.6]

quiz.q: Difference between **multi-admin** and **Joint Fabric**?
quiz.a: **Multi-admin** = one device joins several ecosystems, each keeping its **own separate fabric** (today's reality, ~5 max). **Joint Fabric (1.6)** = multiple controllers **co-administer one fabric** — the structural fix, but spec-now/products-later. [wiki/02 · 1.6]

quiz.q: Which transport is used only for *commissioning*, and which new one joined it in 1.6?
quiz.a: **Bluetooth LE** is commissioning-only (not operational); **NFC** joined as a commissioning transport in 1.6. Operational traffic runs over Thread/Wi-Fi/Ethernet. [wiki/02, wiki/03 · 1.6]

quiz.q: What is a Thread Border Router, why do you need one, and name two examples relevant to Scott.
quiz.a: It **bridges the low-power Thread mesh to your IP network** — without one, Thread devices can't reach the controller. Examples: **Apple HomePod**, **Google Nest Hub**, **Amazon Echo (4th gen)**, **HA Connect/SkyConnect** radio. [wiki/03 · 1.6]

quiz.q: "Works with Matter" ≠ ? — state the gap in one line.
quiz.a: ≠ "all features work." **No platform implements the full spec**; spec → SDK → controller are three clocks that must align; advanced features often fall back to the vendor app. [wiki/04, wiki/06 · 2026]

quiz.q: Name three device categories Matter still doesn't cover (June 2026), and one that *was* a gap but is now covered.
quiz.a: Still missing: **whole-home audio, irrigation, pool/spa**. Now covered (as of 1.5): **cameras** (and closures). [wiki/06, wiki/05 · 1.6]

quiz.q: Which release added the energy devices (solar/battery/heat-pump/EVSE), and which added cameras?
quiz.a: **1.4** (2024-11-07) added energy devices; **1.5** (2025-11-20) added cameras (WebRTC). [wiki/05 · 1.6]

quiz.q: Scott-specific: what new certified bridge connects his lighting layer to Matter, and what must he verify before relying on it?
quiz.a: The **Philips Dynalite Matter Bridge** (CSA-certified) exposes Dynalite/DyNet as Matter endpoints. Verify it **fits his Dynalite generation** and exposes what he needs (scenes/channels) before betting on it. [wiki/03 · 2026]

quiz.q: (Perishable) Which big-four platform is reported *closest* to full Matter coverage, and which *lags*?
quiz.a: **SmartThings** closest; **Google Home** lagging (may miss 1.2-era device types). *Perishable — reverify each refresh.* [wiki/04 · press · 2026]

quiz.q: In a single-surgeon series of >1000 cleft-palate repairs, which repair technique had **zero** postoperative fistulas, and what was the overall fistula rate?
quiz.a: **Furlow palatoplasty** (0/159); overall fistula rate **0.86%** — all fistulas were after straight-line repair. `[157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012549

quiz.q: What is the highest-tier evidence in this issue, and what did it show?
quiz.a: A **double-blind split-wound RCT** of **nanofat** in abdominoplasty (n=50): significantly better POSAS scar scores at 6 months, no oil cysts/fat necrosis. `[L1 · 157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012507

quiz.q: Published formulas for predicting final implant volume in 2-stage TE→implant reconstruction tend to do what?
quiz.a: **Underpredict** final implant size (~23 cc on average; accurate within ~2–4 sizes) — use as a starting point. `[157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012581

quiz.q: For propeller-flap necrosis, which factors predicted risk — and which commonly-assumed factor did **not**?
quiz.a: **Age** and **arc of rotation** (OR 1.01/degree) predicted necrosis; **haemoglobin/haematocrit did NOT**. `[157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012533

quiz.q: Per the issue's nicotine cohort, what should patients be counselled, and why does switching to NRT/e-cigarettes not suffice?
quiz.a: **Complete nicotine abstinence** — wound-complication odds were raised (OR ~5.5–13.5) across **all delivery methods, including NRT and e-cigarettes**. `[157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012547

quiz.q: What classification framework anchors management of vascular anomalies, and what two molecular pathways are the main therapeutic targets?
quiz.a: The **ISSVA** framework (tumours vs malformations); **PI3K/AKT/mTOR** and **RAS/MAPK** — targeted by **sirolimus** and (for infantile haemangioma) **propranolol**. `[CME · 157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012896

quiz.q: What is an Orochi flap, and what problem does it solve?
quiz.a: A secondary free flap anastomosed to a **primary free flap's pedicle** (fabricated chimeric flap) for **vessel-depleted necks**; 0% primary / 4% secondary flap loss, better than vein-grafting. `[157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012557

quiz.q: In sphincter pharyngoplasty, what does an **overlapping** inset trade off against an end-to-end inset?
quiz.a: Better **speech** (balanced resonance 56.5% vs 34.1%) but more **obstructive sleep apnoea** (higher AHI). `[157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012548

quiz.q: For upper-limb lymphedema, which UHFUS measurement point and cutoff best identify disease?
quiz.a: **Proximal volar forearm**; 1.46 mm cutoff (sens 70%/spec 100%); inter-limb difference >0.28 mm identified all cases (100%/100%). `[157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012609

quiz.q: What did the Ontario cost-utility analysis conclude about gender-affirming top surgery?
quiz.a: It was **dominant** vs no surgery — cheaper (−$2,857) and more effective (+0.59 QALY) over 20 years. `[economic · 157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012619

quiz.q: Which novel organism did a 2bRAD-M + murine study implicate in capsular contracture, and what prevention was proposed?
quiz.a: ***Ralstonia pickettii*** (induces thicker, stiffer, more T-cell-infiltrated capsules); **hypochlorous-acid irrigation** proposed. `[preclinical · 157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012570

quiz.q: In the extended transconjunctival lower-lid blepharoplasty series, what unexpected benefit was reported beyond tear-trough correction?
quiz.a: **Predictable improvement of the smile** (from releasing orbicularis origins), with no scleral show, fat necrosis 3.5%, revision 1.0%. `[157(6)] →` https://doi.org/10.1097/PRS.0000000000012545

lessons.title: What Hue is & the two bridges
lessons.body:
**Goal:** by the end you can say what Hue actually is, tell the two bridges apart, and decide whether you need the Pro.

Philips Hue (made by **Signify**) is a **smart-lighting ecosystem**: bulbs, fixtures, lightstrips and accessories that talk to each other over a **Zigbee** mesh — a low-power 2.4 GHz radio, *not* Wi-Fi and *not* Thread. The brain of that mesh is the **Hue Bridge**, a small hub that plugs into your router; it's what your phone app, your voice assistant, and Home Assistant actually talk to. Everything else hangs off the bridge.

There are two current bridges. The **Bridge v2** (the square one, around since 2015) handles up to **50 lights and 12 accessories** — plenty for an accent setup. The newer **Bridge Pro** (launched ~Sept 2025) is a capacity-and-speed upgrade: up to **150+ lights, 50 accessories, 500 scenes**, faster hardware, USB-C and Wi-Fi, plus a Pro-only feature called **SpatialAware**. Crucially, the Pro is **the same Zigbee, the same app, the same API** — it's bigger, not different. (The older round **V1 bridge is end-of-life** — don't build on it.) One nuance to bank: even on the Pro, the legacy **"rules" limit stayed at 250**; the Hue *app* now uses a newer **"scripts"** system (up to 200), so heavy users of third-party apps like iConnectHue can still hit the old rules ceiling.

And one thing that surprises people: since **2024 a Hue account is mandatory** to control your lights through the app. Local control still works, and Home Assistant keeps working after the initial sign-in — but Hue is no longer fully account-free.
lessons.check: *You have about 30 Hue lights and a handful of switches, used as accent lighting. Do you need a Bridge Pro — and would buying one change the protocol your lights use?*

lessons.title: How the bridge is controlled
lessons.body: CLIP API v2 (REST + event-stream push); the Entertainment streaming API (DTLS/UDP); the "don't use REST for fast updates" rule; V2-push vs V1-poll.

lessons.title: Matter & interoperability
lessons.body: What Matter exposure is (and isn't — Zigbee stays internal); the multi-ecosystem picture; the 2026 Matter bugs/fixes; Zigbee/Thread RF coexistence.

lessons.title: Hue under Home Assistant
lessons.body: Native `hue` integration vs Matter (and why native wins for HA); scenes/grouped lights; diyHue/Homebridge; HA-led automation patterns.

lessons.title: Scenes, entertainment & features
lessons.body: Dynamic/gradient scenes; entertainment areas + Sync; SpatialAware; the scripts model.

lessons.title: Limits, migration & staying current
lessons.body: Cloud-account direction; v2-vs-Pro limits + migration gotchas; EOL/deprecations; the **Hue↔Dynalite coexistence** under HA; how this set keeps *you* current.

quiz.q: What radio does Hue use between the bridge and the bulbs — and is Matter that radio?
quiz.a: **Zigbee** (2.4 GHz / 802.15.4). Matter is *not* the radio — it's the *northbound* exposure of the bridge to other ecosystems. [wiki/01, wiki/03 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Three ways the Hue Bridge is controllable on the LAN?
quiz.a: **CLIP REST API v2**, the **local event stream (push)**, and the **Entertainment streaming API** (DTLS/UDP). [wiki/02 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Why is the HA `hue` integration "Local Push" on a V2 bridge but polled on V1?
quiz.a: **V2 bridges (square)** support the **event stream** (push); **V1 bridges (round)** have no push, so they're **polled** — and V1 is EOL. [wiki/02, wiki/04 · 2026.5]

quiz.q: Hue is already in Apple Home via Matter. Best way to also get it into Home Assistant?
quiz.a: The **native `hue` integration** (local CLIP v2, richer entities), **not** via Matter — Matter was the flaky path in 2026. Keep Matter for Apple Home in parallel. [wiki/03, wiki/04 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Bridge Pro vs v2 — lights and accessories limits?
quiz.a: **150+ lights / 50 accessories** (Pro) vs **50 / 12** (v2). Scenes up to 500, scripts up to 200 on Pro. [wiki/01, wiki/06 · 2025-12]

quiz.q: Did the Bridge Pro raise the **rules** limit? Why does that matter?
quiz.a: **No — rules stayed at 250.** Heavy third-party-app users (iConnectHue/All4Hue use the legacy rules engine) can still hit it on a Pro; Hue-app users get the quadrupled **scripts** headroom instead. [wiki/01, wiki/06 · 2025-12]

quiz.q: Name two 2026 Matter problems on Hue and one fix.
quiz.a: e.g. Bridge-Pro **losing its Matter connection** (fixed in fw 2071294010/020, 2026-04-09); HomeKit **"No Response"** when an Apple Home Hub was online; low-brightness lights jumping to full (fixed). [wiki/03 · 2026]

quiz.q: What must you NOT use the REST API for, and what do you use instead?
quiz.a: **Continuous fast light updates** — it's slow, floods the event stream, can't be stopped by other apps. Use the dedicated **Entertainment streaming API** (DTLS/UDP). [wiki/02 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: Where do you create/edit Hue scenes when using Home Assistant?
quiz.a: In the **Hue app** — HA imports them as scene entities and triggers via `hue.activate_scene`; HA can't create/edit Hue scenes. [wiki/04 · 2026.5]

quiz.q: Does Hue still run fully account-free?
quiz.a: **No.** Since **2024** a **Hue account is mandatory** for app control (and for Matter setup). Local control + third-party local controllers still work after sign-in. [wiki/06 · 2024]

quiz.q: How should Hue and Dynalite coexist in Scott's home?
quiz.a: **Complementary, unified at the HA layer** — Dynalite is the wired backbone, Hue the accent layer; HA holds both integrations and coordinates them. Don't make Hue a Dynalite replacement. [wiki/06 · 2026-06]

quiz.q: What's "scripts vs rules" on the Hue bridge?
quiz.a: **Scripts** = the modern automation platform the Hue app uses (≤200 on Pro); **rules** = the legacy engine (≤250) still used by third-party apps. New accessories are scripts-only. [wiki/01 · 2025-12]

lessons.title: What Savant is & the dealer model
lessons.body:
**Goal:** understand why Savant feels expensive and locked-down — and what that means for your decision.

Savant is a premium, professionally-installed automation platform — strong on AV and polish. But it's **dealer-only**: the software that programs it, **RacePoint Blueprint**, is restricted to certified dealers, so you can't change or fix your own system — every adjustment is a paid dealer visit, on top of a subscription. That single fact is why your system feels expensive and why, when it stops working, you're stuck waiting on someone else. It's also the mirror image of Home Assistant, which you run and change yourself for free. Importantly, Savant the *company* isn't dead — it's your *older install* that's failing and uneconomic to keep feeding.
lessons.check: *Why can't you just fix the Savant system yourself, and why does that matter for repair-vs-replace?*

lessons.title: Architecture
lessons.body: da Vinci / Pro Host / Blueprint / Pro App — and what "not working" usually means.

lessons.title: What it controls
lessons.body: The capability inventory = the migration checklist.

lessons.title: Repair vs replace
lessons.body: The honest framework; the dealer-quote-first move; phased migration with AV last.

quiz.q: Savant's three architectural pieces?
quiz.a: **da Vinci** (OS) · **Pro Host** (brain hardware) · **RacePoint Blueprint** (config software). [wiki/02]

quiz.q: Why can't Scott manage his own Savant system?
quiz.a: **RacePoint Blueprint is dealer-only** — homeowners can't program/service it; every change needs a certified dealer. [wiki/01]

quiz.q: Is Savant end-of-life?
quiz.a: **No — the platform is active** (da Vinci, subscription, Savant Power). It's **Scott's older install** that's failing/uneconomic. Distinguish the two. [wiki/01]

quiz.q: The cheap, decisive first step in the repair-vs-replace call?
quiz.a: A **dealer diagnostic/quote** — the one thing Scott can't do himself, and it tells you if the fault is trivial or fundamental. [wiki/04]

quiz.q: Which Savant function carries over to HA most easily, and which is the migration risk?
quiz.a: **Lighting** is easy (Dynalite stays, driven by HA); **AV / multi-room audio** is the make-or-break. [wiki/03, wiki/05]

quiz.q: The core argument for replacing Savant with HA?
quiz.a: **Self-management** — no dealer lock-out, no per-change cost; free software vs subscription + dealer labour. [wiki/04]

quiz.q: What's the obsolescence/version signal for this set?
quiz.a: The **da Vinci OS** version line (e.g. 10.5.1) + the Pro Host model range. [wiki/05]

quiz.q: What happens to this KB if Scott commits to the migration?
quiz.a: It's **designed to go dormant → archive** once Savant is decommissioned (its job done). [wiki/05]

lessons.title: What Thread is & the stack
lessons.body:
**Goal:** by the end you can say what Thread is in one sentence, place it correctly against Matter, and name the mesh roles.

Thread is a **low-power, IPv6, wireless mesh network** for smart-home devices — it runs over **IEEE 802.15.4** radio (the same 2.4 GHz radio family as Zigbee) and uses **6LoWPAN** to squeeze IPv6 into those tiny radio frames, so every Thread device has a real IP address. The single most important idea: **Thread is a *network* layer — it moves packets; it does not decide what a "light" or a "lock" *means*.** That job belongs to **Matter**, which rides *on top* of Thread. So "Thread vs Matter" is a category error — they stack (Matter-over-Thread).

Inside the mesh there's **no single point of failure**: routing is distributed and self-healing. Devices take **dynamic roles** — one elected **Leader**, ordinary **Routers**, **REEDs** (router-eligible end devices that get promoted when more routing is needed), and **End Devices / SEDs** (sleepy battery devices that attach to a parent and never route). Mains-powered devices tend to thicken the mesh as routers; battery devices stay sleepy. A separate box, the **border router**, bridges this mesh to your home network and the internet (that's Lesson 2).
lessons.check: *Someone says "I can't decide between Thread and Matter for my new sensor." What's wrong with the question, and how would you reframe it?*

lessons.title: Border routers & OpenThread
lessons.body: What a BR does; OpenThread (the reference stack) & OTBR; HA's OTBR add-on; running multiple BRs.

lessons.title: Commissioning & credentials
lessons.body: The operational dataset; BLE→join→commission; Thread 1.4 credential sharing; the pending dataset.

lessons.title: Thread vs Matter vs Zigbee
lessons.body: Layer boundaries; Matter rides Thread; Zigbee shares the radio but not the stack; Wi-Fi coexistence; where Dynalite/Hue sit.

lessons.title: Versioning & certification
lessons.body: 1.1→1.4; what 1.4 changed; the Jan 2026 BR certification cutoff; OpenThread reference releases as the obsolescence signal.

lessons.title: Troubleshooting & honest limits
lessons.body: Range, channels, BR sprawl, partitions, diagnostics; what Thread is and isn't good for; how this set keeps *you* current.

quiz.q: What layer is Thread, and what rides on top of it?
quiz.a: Thread is the **network layer** (IPv6 mesh over 802.15.4). **Matter** (the application layer) rides on top — Matter-over-Thread. "Thread vs Matter" is a category error; they stack. [wiki/04 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: Name the radio Thread uses and one protocol that shares it.
quiz.a: **IEEE 802.15.4** at 2.4 GHz (~250 kbit/s, 16 channels). **Zigbee** shares the same radio family. [wiki/01, wiki/04 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: How is Thread different from Zigbee despite the shared radio?
quiz.a: **Thread is natively IPv6** (every device IP-routable). **Zigbee** has its own non-IP app stack and needs a **translating gateway/bridge** (e.g. a Hue Bridge). [wiki/04 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: What does a Thread **border router** do, and can you run more than one?
quiz.a: Bridges the mesh to your LAN/internet (incl. NAT64/DNS64, DHCPv6-PD). **Yes — run multiple BRs on one shared network** for redundancy. [wiki/02 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: Name the four core device roles in a Thread mesh.
quiz.a: **Leader** (one, elected) · **Router** · **REED** (router-eligible end device, promotable) · **End Device / SED** (sleepy, attaches to a parent, never routes). [wiki/01 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: What is the **operational dataset**, and which field is the secret?
quiz.a: The TLV bundle defining a Thread network (PAN ID, XPAN, Channel, Network Name, etc.). The **Network Key** is the secret credential — possessing it = ability to join. [wiki/03 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: When was Thread **1.4** released and what's its headline feature?
quiz.a: **September 2024**; headline = **credential sharing** — a new BR joins the *existing* mesh instead of creating a competing one. [wiki/05, wiki/03 · 2024-09]

quiz.q: As of **1 Jan 2026**, what spec must a NEW border router be certified to?
quiz.a: **Thread 1.4** — 1.3 BR certification applications closed 31 Dec 2025; 1.4 is the only certifiable BR spec now. (Existing 1.3 hardware keeps working.) [wiki/05 · 2026-01]

quiz.q: Is Thread 1.4 credential sharing usable in **Home Assistant** today?
quiz.a: **Not yet via the standard.** HA's OTBR add-on (v2.16.0) runs the **1.4 core in beta**, but **credential sharing is not yet exposed** in HA. [wiki/02, wiki/03 · 2026-01]

quiz.q: Your Thread mesh is flaky — what do you check first?
quiz.a: **RF coexistence / channel plan.** Thread/Zigbee use narrow 2 MHz channels that overlap Wi-Fi's wide channels; move Thread into the gaps. [wiki/06, wiki/04 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: What's the safe way to change a Thread network's credentials/channel without dropping it?
quiz.a: The **Pending Operational Dataset** with a delay timer — all devices switch together at expiry. [wiki/03 · 2026.6]

quiz.q: What's the cleanest **obsolescence signal** for which spec level a stack is qualified at?
quiz.a: **OpenThread reference releases** (`ot-reference-release`) tagged against a **Thread Test Harness** version. [wiki/05 · 2026.6]

lessons.title: Lesson 1
lessons.body:
`ORC:` = **Orc**hestrator (sits with your `PMT:` / `SQ:` prefixes). You type `ORC: <rough ask>` and it hands back a **scoped, routed, ready-to-run job**.

Two things define it:

1. **It's a pre-flight router that STOPS.** It refines your ask, attaches the right folders/zone, checks no other chat is already doing it, recommends *where* it should run (a fresh chat, a sub-agent, or a panel), then produces the artifact and **stops for your go.** It never launches a fresh chat or executes on its own.
2. **It's recommend-only (procedural, not epistemic).** It routes and scopes; it never overrides the actual worker's judgement. Its route is a recommendation you (or the worker) can overrule.

Mental model: ORC: is a **switchboard**, not a boss. It points a rough ask at the right room with the right scope — it doesn't do the work itself.

---

lessons.title: Lesson 2
lessons.body:
**Use `ORC:`** when you're *starting a new, rough, possibly-big or possibly-duplicative chunk of work* and you'd want it scoped and routed before diving in.

**Skip it** for:
- a quick factual question,
- continuing work already underway in a chat,
- plain conversation,
- a task that's already well-specified.

**The test:** *"Am I launching a new piece of work I'd want scoped before diving in?"* — Yes → `ORC:`. No → just ask.

A wrong call is cheap: if you ORC: something trivial, it just says "this was overkill — just do it here." So when in doubt, it's safe to try.

---

lessons.title: Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Your commands fall into three families:

- **Pre-flight / input-shaping** — `ORC:`, `PMT:`, `SQ:`. They turn a rough input into a well-formed one, then **STOP** (you act).
- **Worker skills** — `create-academic-set` (CAS), library-retrieval, book-to-expert, etc. They *do* the job.
- **Transition / persistence** — `HO`, `ship`, `checkpoint`, `park:`. They move or save state.

Each pre-flight command shapes a different thing: `SQ:` sharpens the **question**, `PMT:` drafts the **prompt**, `ORC:` refines **+ scopes + routes + duplicate-checks** and emits the **opener**. ORC: is the broadest.

**The one rule: one front door per job.** Pick a single entry command for a given move — don't chain the pre-flight ones on the same raw input. ORC: is the most managerial: it can hand off *to* a PMT or CAS step in the next chat.

---

lessons.title: Lesson 4
lessons.body:
- **`ORC:` + `PMT:`** — *don't run both on the same raw ask* (both ask clarifying questions — redundant). Use them sequentially across chats: `ORC:` picks the room and gives you the opener → in the new chat, `PMT:` writes the detailed brief. **ORC picks the room; PMT furnishes it.**
- **`ORC:` + `PMT-CAS` + `CAS`** — yes, this works, as a clean 3-stage chain. `ORC:` recognises an academic-set job and **routes** it (fresh chat, general zone, Second Brain folders, research defaults) → `PMT-CAS` drafts the exact CAS prompt → `CAS` executes. ORC sets CAS up; it doesn't replace it.
- **`ORC:` + `HO`** — *don't combine.* `HO` continues the **same** thread and already carries the context the next chat needs; `ORC:` shapes a **new** ask. They're cousins (both produce a "start the next chat with this" artifact), which is exactly why you pick one per transition. The only time both appear: mid-thread you spin off a genuinely *separate* sub-job → `ORC:` that, and `HO` the main thread if you're pausing it.

---

lessons.title: Lesson 5
lessons.body:
There's no background "ORC engine" — it's a skill that loads when you invoke it. Most of its good behaviours (duplicate-check, folder least-privilege, sharpening, escalate-when-unsure) **already fire automatically every chat** as standing conventions. ORC: bundles them deliberately, up front, and adds the portable **opener** for a fresh chat.

Auto-running it on every message would gate everything behind a refine-and-confirm step (friction, and it trains reflexive OK-clicking), and it can't read your intent reliably. The planned middle ground is **auto-*suggest*** — Claude offers "want me to ORC: this first?" on a big, sprawling, underspecified ask — never auto-run. You stay in control of the front door.

---

lessons.title: Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Lesson 5 ended on the **opener** — the portable one-liner that starts a fresh chat. This lesson makes the everyday mechanic concrete, because it applies to *any* good prompt, not just an `ORC:` job.

Whenever a prompt earns its keep, Claude offers to **save** it to `prompts/` (with an `INDEX.md` row). But a saved prompt is dead weight if you don't know where to run it or have to re-paste the whole thing. So a save-offer now always comes with **two more things**:

1. **A venue recommendation (RR-led): here, or a fresh chat?** The default is a **fresh chat** when the job is long, tool-heavy, or in a different zone — that keeps your drafting/router seat clean and quarantines the run's context. **Here** is the default only when you're continuing work already in flight. Claude states its pick before asking.
2. **The paste-ready opener** — one fenced code block, one-click copy — pointing the new chat at the saved file: `Read Projects/Claude/prompts/<slug>.md and run the prompt in it.` Plus a reminder of which **folders/zone** that fresh chat will need to mount (from the prompt's `Folders:` line), so the restart doesn't dead-end on access.

**The trap this closes:** confusing the opener with `WWW`. `WWW` *resumes a paused thread* from memory — it can't start a saved prompt, because the prompt lives in `prompts/`, not memory. The opener is the right tool for a **new run from a saved file**; `WWW` is for **picking up where a thread left off**. Different jobs, different triggers.

Mental model: **save = shelve the recipe; opener = the address of the kitchen to cook it in.** A good save hands you both.

---

*Next: do `quiz.md`. Pass mark = 11/13, with the pairing questions (9–12) all correct.*

quiz.q: Fresh chat: "What's the maximum oral rosuvastatin dose per the TGA PI?" — you just want the number. Which front door?
quiz.a: Just ask (no command). A quick factual lookup isn't a new job to route — the test ("am I launching work I'd want scoped?") is No.

quiz.q: "I want to do something with all my old operation notes — not sure what yet, could be big." You want it scoped before committing. Which front door?
quiz.a: `ORC:`. Rough, new, potentially big, scope-before-committing — textbook Orchestrator.

quiz.q: You're 40 messages into building a spreadsheet and want to keep going on that same spreadsheet. Which front door?
quiz.a: Just continue (no command). ORC: is pre-flight — it shapes the start of a new job, not work already in flight.

quiz.q: You `ORC:` a one-line task and it replies "this was overkill — just do it here." What does that mean?
quiz.a: The built-in "no new route" branch fired — the ask was too small to need routing, which is why a wrong ORC: call is cheap.

quiz.q: You want a sharper version of a fuzzy question to take to a colleague — no job, no routing. Which front door?
quiz.a: `SQ:`. It sharpens the question itself; ORC: would be over-kit.

quiz.q: What does `ORC:` add over `PMT:`?
quiz.a: It scopes folders/zone, routes where the work runs, and duplicate-checks; PMT only drafts a prompt. ORC: is the broadest pre-flight wrapper.

quiz.q: True or false: typing `ORC:` is required for a new chat to load your router and function properly.
quiz.a: False. The global field loads automatically at chat birth regardless; the ORC: opener is an optional extra layer, never a prerequisite.

quiz.q: You're starting a new workstream but worry another chat may already be on it. Which command's pipeline includes the duplicate-work check?
quiz.a: `ORC:`. The cross-chat duplicate check is part of its pipeline (PMT/SQ/HO don't do it).

quiz.q: You want to build a brand-new academic set on a topic, done properly. What's the right chain?
quiz.a: `ORC:` (route) → `PMT-CAS` (prompt) → `CAS` (execute). ORC sets CAS up; it doesn't replace it.

quiz.q: You're pausing a long thread so a fresh chat can continue the same work, and you're tempted to also `ORC:` it. Best move?
quiz.a: `HO` only. It already carries the context; ORC:-ing a handoff is redundant.

quiz.q: You run `PMT:` on a rough ask, then `ORC:` on the same raw ask. What's wrong?
quiz.a: You chained two pre-flight commands on one input — both ask clarifiers, so it's redundant. One front door per job.

quiz.q: Mid-thread on Project X you find a genuinely separate new sub-job worth its own chat, and you also want to pause Project X. Which combination makes sense?
quiz.a: `ORC:` the new sub-job, and `HO` the Project X thread you're pausing — the one case where both legitimately appear.

quiz.q: You've drafted a strong prompt for a long, multi-source job and Claude offers to save it. Beyond saving the file, what should the offer include — and how do you start the run?
quiz.a: A venue recommendation (RR: a fresh chat for a long/tool-heavy job) plus a paste-ready one-liner opener pointing the new chat at the saved file — not `WWW`, which only resumes a paused thread from memory.
